items.title: Hydra rebuilds the body by restoring its internal cellular map
items.summary: Before a hydra can regrow a head or foot, its cells appear to rebuild the internal map that tells them where they are and what they should become next, FMI researchers report. The work suggests that cells need to interpret not only where they are in the body, but also what stage of regeneration they

items.title: Floating colonies: Engineering 3D microbial architectures in liquid cultures
items.summary: Microbes in the gut, lungs and biofilms do not simply exist as perfectly mixed populations; instead, they grow in layered and clustered three-dimensional (3D) arrangements. This spatial organization shapes how nutrients and chemical signals move between neighboring cells and how much oxygen differen

items.title: Reflooding the Aral Sea could prevent massive CO₂ emissions
items.summary: About 90% of the Aral Sea has dried up. The exposed sediments have already released enormous amounts of carbon dioxide (748 megatons) since 1960. However, through restoration, the lake could once again become a carbon sink. This is shown by a study by the Centre for Advanced Studies of Blanes (CEAB-

items.title: Researchers expand simulation tool to help design the next generation of photonic and quantum devices
items.summary: Many modern technologies, from optical communications and artificial intelligence (AI) hardware to advanced sensors and medical imaging, depend on photonic and semiconductor devices that precisely control the interaction between light and electrons. Designing these devices, however, remains a major

items.title: Lab-grown mini brains may predict which Alzheimer’s treatments will work
items.summary: Miniature brain models grown from patients’ cells revealed striking differences in how Alzheimer’s-related tissue responds to an antidepressant. The organoids and the particles they release could eventually guide more personalized treatments and provide new clues for diagnosing the disease.

items.title: Early respiratory infections may lower higher education completion and adult income
items.summary: Infants exposed to respiratory illnesses through older siblings typically fare worse in education, income and health in adulthood, according to new research from the University of Copenhagen.

items.title: Children become 'climate detectives' to help cool overheating schools
items.summary: Elementary school children equipped with thermal cameras are helping redesign their schools to cope with hotter summers, thanks to a University of Surrey program that gives pupils the tools to spot dangerous heat hotspots and develop their own cooling solutions.

items.title: A strange quantum effect dramatically boosts energy transfer
items.summary: Researchers discovered a proton-assisted mechanism that greatly improves how triplet energy moves between quantum dots and nearby molecules. The proton briefly shifts position, helps coordinate electron movement, and then returns to where it started. This quantum-driven shuttle could offer a powerfu

items.title: In Kyushu's decorated tombs, humans were never meant to be individuals
items.summary: When you first step into Japan's Kyushu tombs, the first thing you notice is nothing at all. "When you enter, you're engulfed by darkness; you can smell earth and stone, and you have the sensation of being enclosed …," said Claudia Zancan, a Ph.D. candidate who examined these sixth to seve

items.title: Can hedging make you a better communicator?
items.summary: "People hedge all the time," says Wharton marketing professor Jonah Berger. "Whether we are talking to a boss or a colleague, a salesperson talking to a client, a marketer trying to pitch something or even talking to family members, we often say things like 'That could work' or 'This

items.title: Why a tiny social media post has mathematicians rethinking AI
items.summary: As millions of people were coming down from the excitement of the FIFA World Cup final at the start of this week, a different kind of excitement was building within the mathematical community. Levent Alpöge, a mathematician who works at the artificial intelligence (AI) company Anthropic, casually an

items.title: Scientists turn radio telescopes into space scanners—sharpening view of hidden orbital threats
items.summary: Scientists have successfully demonstrated a breakthrough in how sensitive radio telescopes can be used to significantly improve the world's ability to detect, track and characterize satellites and space debris in orbit.

items.title: New review highlights gaps in care for First Nations children in Canada
items.summary: A new study finds that First Nations children still face long-standing inequalities in health care access in Canada despite improvements made through the application of Jordan's Principle—a policy designed to ensure First Nations children receive the services they need. The work is published in

items.title: Q&A: What happens when producers are responsible for recycling their products and packaging?
items.summary: Who should pay when a product becomes waste—the company that made it or the public? That question sits at the center of extended producer responsibility (EPR), a policy strategy that shifts recycling costs onto manufacturers to reduce waste and promote more eco-friendly design. Used in more than 100

items.title: New multiplexing scheme accelerates long-distance quantum communication
items.summary: Quantum networks, systems consisting of multiple connected nodes or devices that can transmit quantum information to one another, have the potential to advance future communications. These networks typically leverage entanglement, a quantum phenomenon that prompts two or more distant particles to be

items.title: MIT’s new lidar chip could give self-driving cars a wider view
items.summary: MIT engineers have found a way to give chip-based lidar a wider, clearer view without relying on moving parts. Their design uses differently shaped antennas that can sit close together without scrambling one another’s signals. In tests, the system sharply reduced interference while steering a single

items.title: Reversing soil degradation by 10% could feed 70 million more, global analysis finds
items.summary: Factors such as erosion, dehydration and soil compaction are jeopardizing food supplies for broad swaths of the world's population, according to a study by the University of Bonn, the University of Minnesota, the University of Basel and Hamburg University of Technology. If human-made soil degra

items.title: Nearly 3,000-year-old human remains unearthed in El Salvador
items.summary: Archaeologists in El Salvador exhumed human remains believed to be nearly 3,000 years old Tuesday, the Ministry of Culture said.

items.title: Danish fish fossil cliffs set to gain UNESCO status
items.summary: Two Danish coastal cliffs where paleontologists have uncovered 55-million-year-old fossils of prehistoric wildlife are likely to gain World Heritage status as UNESCO's committee meets in South Korea.

items.title: Private mission launches to extend life of out-of-gas communication satellites
items.summary: A two-armed space robot blasted off on a private salvage operation Tuesday to slap life-extending jetpacks on old satellites running low on fuel thousands of miles up.

items.title: Japan cities 'cruelly hot' for first time
items.summary: Japanese cities are experiencing "cruelly hot days," a new meteorological term devised this year to warn people of intense heat above 40°C (104°F), with heatstroke alerts issued across nearly the entire country.

items.title: 2,000 volunteers join a painstaking effort to restore massive skeleton of prehistoric predator
items.summary: Volunteers armed with chisels, brushes and protective goggles chip away at rock encasing the bones of a massive prehistoric amphibian in a glass-walled laboratory on the ground floor of Warsaw's Copernicus Science Centre.

items.title: Scientists revive a powerful antibiotic that superbugs had defeated
items.summary: Scientists have found a way to revive vancomycin, a powerful antibiotic that some dangerous bacteria have learned to resist. Instead of creating an entirely new drug, researchers paired vancomycin with a small molecule called pghi-4, which blocks a bacterial enzyme linked to resistance. The combinat

items.title: Analysis suggests debt may increase suicide risk
items.summary: In a study published in Economic Inquiry that analyzed debt and income across U.S. counties at the onset of the Great Recession in 2008, investigators uncovered evidence that debt may affect the likelihood of suicide.

items.title: Factors that affect the spininess of plants in different locations
items.summary: Plants often grow spines, thorns and prickles to defend against animals that feed on them, but why this happens in some places more than others has remained a mystery. Research in New Phytologist shows that plant spininess is shaped by browsing mammals, both past and present, together with environme

items.title: Advance could enable enzymatic reactions for chemical manufacturing beyond the boiling point of water
items.summary: Enzymes are proteins that can be harnessed for chemical manufacturing, but they are typically restricted to mild temperatures because proteins unfold and lose their function under heat. In new research published in Angewandte Chemie Novit, investigators found that a porous organic polymer can be use

items.title: Circulating microRNAs offer insights for detecting and treating cachexia in senior dogs
items.summary: Cachexia—a syndrome characterized by progressive muscle wasting—affects both humans and dogs with chronic diseases such as cancer. A recent study in Molecular Oncology found that lower circulating levels of certain microRNAs—small noncoding RNAs that regulate gene expression—often indicate the prese

items.title: Amerika nije imala ovoliko slučajeva ospica u 35 godina
items.summary: BROJ slučajeva ospica u SAD-u dosegnuo je 35-godišnji maksimum, premašivši brojke iz 2025. Stručnjaci za to krive pad procijepljenosti, dezinformacije i kolektivni zaborav opasnosti bolesti.

items.title: Magic mushrooms may reshape the brain long after the trip ends
items.summary: A single dose of psilocybin produced measurable changes in brain activity and possible changes in brain structure that lasted for up to a month. Participants who experienced the greatest increase in flexible, varied brain activity also reported more personal insight and later improvements in well-be

items.title: Australian cave discovery shows grass was burned for magic, cures and curses for 25,000 years
items.summary: The GunaiKurnai, an Australian Aboriginal nation, are the acknowledged traditional custodians of Gippsland and the southern Victorian Alps. Within their territory are caves known from 19th-century ethnographic reports and oral traditions to have been settings for magic, healing and cursing rituals b

items.title: A Week of Smoky Skies Across North America
items.summary: Brown carbon, emitted by wildland fires and tracked over the span of a week in July 2026, worsened air quality in parts of the U.S. and Canada.

items.title: Droughts can boost inclusive institutions, social unity
items.summary: Droughts have an immediate negative impact on agricultural production, but they can also trigger institutional and cultural transformations that help make societies more resilient to environmental crises. This is the main conclusion of the article "Climate shocks, democratization and (a culture of)

items.title: Why low-skilled workers are less likely to participate in continuing education
items.summary: Low-skilled workers are less likely to participate in employer-sponsored continuing education programs than their more highly skilled colleagues. They are less convinced that acquiring additional skills has a positive effect on career success and job satisfaction. This is the finding of a survey con

items.title: New research sheds light on Texas hill country floods
items.summary: Two companion papers in the Bulletin of the American Meteorological Society provide new insight into the causes and predictability of the 2025 Texas Hill Country floods, the deadliest flash flood event in the United States since 1976.

items.title: Rapid bacterial community profiling of equine microbiomes using Oxford Nanopore sequencing
items.summary: Members of the Horse Microbiome Research Group recently published a new study in the Journal of Medical Microbiology, led by Dr. Joy Leng, who is currently undertaking the Alborada Well Foal 2 project. This research aims to understand how the foal gut microbiome develops by investigating the microbi

items.title: Electyrolyzer experiment designated ready-to-fly by NASA
items.summary: A Southwest Research Institute and University of Texas at San Antonio experiment has passed a critical NASA review, designating it ready for parabolic flight testing. The two institutions will evaluate the performance of a patent-pending electrolyzer, the Mars Atmospheric Reactor for Synthesis of Co

items.title: Does dark energy really exist? Our work identifies cracks in the foundations of today's cosmological model
items.summary: According to current understanding, the universe is expanding—and is doing so at an accelerating rate. This is believed to be caused by something called the cosmological constant, which was first proposed by Albert Einstein in his theory of general relativity. In recent decades, it has become better

items.title: Coffee may protect your heart but energy drinks could harm it
items.summary: For most adults, a daily caffeine habit may be safer for the heart than many people assume. A new American Heart Association review found that up to 400 milligrams of caffeine a day, roughly three to five cups of regular black coffee, is generally safe and may even be linked to lower risks of heart

items.title: Leveraging social expectation for charitable donations
items.summary: Avoiding guilt is a powerful motivator for charitable giving, according to a study in PNAS Nexus. Masahiko Haruno and colleagues ran two experiments to determine the most effective psychological framing to encourage donations to WaterAid, an NGO working to provide clean water and sanitation worldwid

items.title: Birds flying in 'V' formation save energy with flatter flaps, new research shows
items.summary: Scientists have known for years that birds like geese and ibises gain an aerodynamic advantage from flying in a "V" formation. Now, researchers from Brown University have provided new insights into the nature of that advantage.

items.title: Urgent cuts to global greenhouse gas emissions needed to avoid worst sea-level rise flooding in the UK, study shows
items.summary: A pioneering study has revealed that by the turn of the next century, at least half a million more people in the UK will be at risk of coastal flooding—a 25% increase compared with now. The research, led by the Met Office and the University of Bristol, quantifies for the first time the scale of the

items.title: Breastfeeding rather than bottle-feeding is seen as the ideal of a good mother in Instagram comments
items.summary: A study by Pompeu Fabra University (UPF) analyzes Instagram discourse that associates the ideal of being a good mother with exclusive breastfeeding and fosters guilt among women who cannot or do not wish to breastfeed, often because of the difficulty of balancing it with other daily responsibilities

items.title: NISAR’s L-Band Radar Reveals ‘Hummingbird’ in Antarctica
items.summary: Description Data from the Earth-orbiting U.S.-India NISAR (NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar) satellite’s L-band radar was used to produce an image of Nunatak Zaterjavshijsja — a mountaintop in East Antarctica — poking out amid a stream of ice flowing northeast to the ocean. The obstruction causes

items.title: Testing innovation speeds up detection of deadly tick-borne disease
items.summary: A new method at the University of Missouri could transform how Rocky Mountain spotted fever is diagnosed. The method detects the deadly tick-borne disease in just 40 minutes—a leap that could help patients get lifesaving treatment sooner. The study, "A rapid isothermal RPA–CRISPR/Cas12a assay for de

items.title: UN report: Global hunger levels ease for third consecutive year as regional disparities persist
items.summary: Global hunger declined for a third consecutive year in 2025, demonstrating that progress is possible. However, the improvements remain fragile, unevenly distributed and insufficient to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals by 2030, according to The State of Food Security and Nutrition in the Wor

items.title: Not so terrible, after all: Two‑year‑olds just want to be independent
items.summary: A 2-year-old is lying on the floor yelling, with their socks pulled off after spending 15 minutes trying to get them on in the first place. A caregiver might take a deep breath and think, "Here we go again—another 'terrible twos' tantrum."

items.title: Ruthenium nanoparticles convert captured perchlorate into harmless chloride in water treatment waste
items.summary: For decades, water utilities have relied on giant tanks filled with ion-exchange resin beads to remove perchlorate, a harmful industrial pollutant, from drinking water. The system works by attracting negatively charged perchlorate ions to positively charged resin beads, allowing purified water to fl

items.title: Research explains psychology of helping others feel better about personal disappointments
items.summary: Personal and professional conversations about triumph and disappointment happen every day, but what is the most appropriate way to react when others share their news with us? New research by Bayes Business School (City St George's, University of London) reveals that a common reaction is to mirr

items.title: Gig platforms can simply 'wait out' workers to slash wages, research reveals
items.summary: Researchers from the Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems (MPI-IS), the Tübingen AI Center and Ellis Institute Tübingen have conducted a study that uncovers a mathematical strategy explaining how digital labor platforms can systematically suppress wages. The research, titled "Stochastic Wage

items.title: New study into humans' closest ape relatives suggests physical touch can promote social harmony
items.summary: Physical touch may be an important evolutionary tool to help manage social tension and keep the peace. That is according to a new study led by Durham University in the UK, analyzing behaviors of our closest ape relatives, bonobos and chimpanzees.

items.title: Mysterious gas 'bullets' race from Milky Way's only known helium nova at up to 20 million mph
items.summary: Mysterious high-speed "bullets"—clumps of possibly oxygen-rich gas traveling at up to 20 million miles per hour—have been discovered shooting out of the rarest stellar explosion in our galaxy.

items.title: Legalization of sports betting has led to households saving significantly less money
items.summary: As legalized sports betting becomes increasingly accessible through mobile apps such as DraftKings and FanDuel, a new study by BYU finance professors finds that people are saving less to fuel gambling habits.

items.title: Satellite imagery captures the impact of large U.S. dams on river temperatures over the last decade
items.summary: A team of Virginia Tech geoscientists has found a new way to examine the widespread effects of dams on river temperatures: using Landsat thermal infrared imagery to gather data on rivers across the United States. By using satellite imagery instead of site-specific, on-the-ground measurements, resear

items.title: NASA to Host Media Briefing on Roman Telescope, Launching Next Month
items.summary: Media are invited to join NASA for a virtual news conference at 2 p.m. EDT, Wednesday, July 29, to preview the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope mission, scheduled to launch from the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida on Sunday, Aug. 30. NASA will stream this event live through a variety of pl

items.title: Novel antibiotic candidates starve resistant bacteria by blocking vitamin supply
items.summary: Bacteria have spent decades evolving resistance to virtually every antibiotic we have thrown at them. To stay ahead, new drugs must hit targets that existing antibiotics have never touched. A research team at the Helmholtz Institute for Pharmaceutical Research Saarland (HIPS) has now developed a ser

items.title: Researcher shows how AI can personalize learning for homeschooling families
items.summary: More than 3.4 million American children, or about 6% of the school-age population, now learn at home, and that group has grown at nearly three times the pre-pandemic rate. Homeschooling is also increasingly popular in countries including Canada and the United Kingdom, as more parents look to customi

items.title: Atomic channels can separate rare earth elements from each other without using toxic chemicals
items.summary: Rare earth elements like lanthanum, neodymium and dysprosium are used to build the electric motor in your car, the LED lights in your house and the MRI machine at your doctor's office. But first, they have to be mined and separated from each other. Historically, that purification has been a dif

items.title: Researchers develop automated system to accelerate outbreak detection and disease surveillance
items.summary: Biomedical engineers at Brown University have developed a fully automated workflow that simplifies and accelerates the preparation of bacterial samples for genetic sequencing. The method, dubbed Pathogen2Read, streamlines a critical bottleneck in the genetic sequencing process and could enable small

items.title: Animals in prehistoric paintings reveal hidden pattern across nine Basque caves
items.summary: Iñaki Intxaurbe, a researcher at the EHU-University of the Basque Country, analyzed the distribution of images in nine caves in the Basque Country using network-based analysis and statistical techniques.

items.title: Family courts must do more to hear children's voices, study says
items.summary: Children involved in private family law proceedings in England can experience serious harm to their mental and physical health because their voices are too often ignored or dismissed, according to new research led by the University of Manchester in partnership with SHERA Research Group members and T

items.title: Human eyes needed, not AI, to study the Arctic sky
items.summary: Scientists studying space weather are turning to human volunteers rather than artificial intelligence to help sort through hundreds of thousands of images of the Arctic sky. The Clear Skies—Kiruna project is asking members of the public to look at pictures of the sky above Kiruna, Sweden, and say wh

items.title: Sleeping sickness parasite reveals enzyme weak spot that could guide safer drugs
items.summary: African sleeping sickness and related diseases can be treated only with toxic and sometimes ineffective drugs. Now, University of Connecticut researchers show in Nucleic Acids Research that a crucial enzyme could be the key to safer, more effective medicines.

items.title: As Supreme Court decisions grow sharper, new study examines justices' calculated concessions
items.summary: Recent U.S. Supreme Court terms have seen justices openly accuse one another of rewriting statutes, ignoring precedent and substituting policy preferences for law. Whether in disputes over presidential immunity, administrative power, nationwide injunctions or transgender rights, justices have demons

items.title: Understudied dogs may reshape what science knows about canine behavior
items.summary: Dogs are humans' best friends in many places across the globe. But does a dog in New York City respond in the same way as a dog in Kyoto or Buenos Aires?

items.title: Extreme precipitation in Central Asia connected to drought in mainland Southeast Asia
items.summary: In much of Asia, the effects of climate change are felt through an increased frequency of extreme weather events, especially water-related ones. Droughts are becoming more common in mainland Southeast Asia, while periods with unusually large amounts of rainfall are increasing in Central Asia. Rice U

items.title: Fragmented system hampering UK response to economic crime, researchers find
items.summary: A new study from The University of Manchester has offered the most comprehensive look to date at how the U.K. coordinates its response to economic crime, identifying the structural challenges that make this difficult to do well. The study examines the tensions that make these challenges resistant to

items.title: Vapor-controlled supramolecules can switch color, glow and phase
items.summary: Controlling interactions between molecules and their functions from the molecular level to the macroscopic scale is challenging in supramolecular and materials chemistry. To this end, a research team led by associate professor Yosuke Tani from WPI-ITbM at Nagoya University and Keisuke Wada from Kyot

items.title: Study finds river sand mining is outstripping nature's supply
items.summary: The sand used to build cities, roads and other infrastructure is being removed from many rivers faster than nature can replace it, according to a global review led by researchers from Nanyang Technological University, Singapore (NTU Singapore).

items.title: New radio-burst method helps locate universe's missing ordinary matter
items.summary: Stars and galaxies make up much of the universe's ordinary, observable matter. But for decades, scientists have wrestled with a cosmic conflict: There should be much more.

items.title: Researchers call for a shift in crop development to tackle hunger, malnutrition, and climate change
items.summary: Feeding a growing global population is no longer enough. Scientists say the next generation of crops must also deliver better nutrition while withstanding the increasing impacts of climate change.

items.title: Hawaiian stilt reclassified from endangered to threatened after 50 years of conservation efforts
items.summary: The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service announced the reclassification of the Aʻeo, or Hawaiian stilt (Himantopus mexicanus knudseni), from endangered to threatened under the Endangered Species Act, a rare and hopeful milestone for a native Hawaiian species. The change, published in the Federal Register

items.title: US-India Satellite Delivers Data, Reveals ‘Hummingbird’ in Antarctica
items.summary: As of July 20, the public can access data from the two powerful radar instruments aboard the NISAR (NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar) satellite. Teams in the United States and India will release files processed from the satellite’s L-band and S-band radars on an ongoing basis, helping researchers

items.title: New insights into how DNA, the body's instruction manual for life, is regulated
items.summary: Scientists at the Peter MacCallum Cancer Center have uncovered new insights into how cells regulate DNA methylation, a fundamental process that helps keep potentially harmful viral elements in our genome under control.

items.title: NASA Langley Celebrates Community through Music with ‘Symphony Under the Stars’ Event
items.summary: NASA Langley’s “Stars, Stripes, and Supernovas – Symphony Under the Stars” event brought employees, families, and community members together for an evening filled with music, connection, and celebration.   Organizers planned the event, which took place July 16, to mark the 250th&

items.title: A new compact instrument enables high-fidelity measurements of energetic particles on CubeSats
items.summary: A team of NASA-sponsored scientists and engineers has developed a novel approach to observing high-energy particles in the near-Earth space environment, incorporating miniaturized sensors into a compact, multiview particle-detection instrument unlike any before it.

items.title: Frozen fiber couples light and sound 1,000 times more strongly than standard glass fibers
items.summary: Researchers have developed a new type of optical fiber by freezing a glass capillary filled with liquid. It guides light and sound waves simultaneously and enables highly efficient coupling between them. The high coupling strength lowers the energy consumption of photonic neuromorphic computing sche

items.title: Study shows nutrient loss in vineyards can be attributed to factors beyond fruit harvest
items.summary: New research from Washington State University could help grape growers improve nutrient use efficiency and develop a more accurate fertilizer budget, helping ensure high fruit quality, vineyard productivity and long-term economic and environmental sustainability.

items.title: New exhibition reveals 10,000 years of prehistoric life in the Trent Valley
items.summary: A new exhibition at the University of Nottingham Museum is bringing the prehistoric story of the Trent Valley to life, showcasing archaeological discoveries that are transforming understanding of one of Britain's most significant river landscapes.

items.title: Zoo elephant survival has improved substantially over 6 decades, international study finds
items.summary: Elephants living in modern zoos are living significantly longer than they did half a century ago, according to a study published in the journal Scientific Reports. The research—led by ZSL (the Zoological Society of London) in collaboration with Species360, the University of Southern Denmark and the

items.title: Urgent action is needed to protect Somalia from a potentially unprecedented weather event, experts warn
items.summary: Cardiff University leads a multidisciplinary research collaboration representing academia and multiple nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) to explore Somalia's 2023 El Niño floods and lessons learned from a similar event in 1997. The research, which has yet to undergo peer review, was released

items.title: Antarctic 12-m Terahertz Telescope project to uncover hidden galaxy formation
items.summary: A new simulation study suggests that the Antarctic 12-m Terahertz Telescope (ATT12), a next-generation facility led by University of Tsukuba, could uncover between 1 and 10 million dusty star-forming galaxies throughout cosmic history.

items.title: Znanstvenici: Naša galaksija možda se okrenula na bok
items.summary: NOVE simulacije pokazuju da se disk Mliječne staze možda nagnuo na bok nakon sudara s drugom galaksijom. Ovaj događaj objašnjava zašto zvijezde u halou naše galaksije rotiraju sporije od očekivanog.

items.title: Low cloud-cover has helped mute warming for the last 47 years, but models predict this will change
items.summary: A recent study, published in Geophysical Research Letters, combines historical satellite observations with atmospheric reanalysis data to construct a multidecade record of low-cloud cover in the hope of improving current climate models.

items.title: Record fires killed nearly 1 in 5 mature giant sequoias, but controlled burns can boost survival
items.summary: A new study found that giant sequoias were nearly four times as likely to survive extreme wildfires in forests managed with prescribed burns, which decrease tree mortality. The work is published in the journal Nature Communications.

items.title: Is there really a global loneliness epidemic? Research reveals a more complex picture
items.summary: Few human experiences are as universal—or as poorly understood—as loneliness. More than just feeling blue and the inspiration for countless songs, it's linked to poorer well-being, worse mental and physical health and a higher risk of early death.

items.title: Reinventing food packaging with safe and sustainable oxygen-scavenging biodegradable plastic
items.summary: From ensuring that food remains fresh longer to extending its shelf life for future consumption, food packaging plays an indispensable role in modern society. Conventional food packaging plastics are sturdy and highly effective at keeping out oxygen, one of the main drivers of food spoilage.

items.title: When a SpaceX rocket crashes into the moon, scientists will be watching
items.summary: On Aug. 5, 2026, an abandoned piece of space junk is predicted to crash into the moon near a spot called Einstein Crater. Astronomers will be watching because the collision and aftermath may be visible from Earth.

items.title: How sea anemone cells reform into an organism
items.summary: Researchers at the University of Vienna have discovered a key mechanism that enables sea anemones to regenerate into fully developed organisms from disorganized clusters of cells. The study, published in Nature Communications, shows that the so-called Notch signaling pathway controls tissue organiza

items.title: Google wants to release 16 million sterile mosquitoes: Australian tests offer a valuable lesson
items.summary: In the United States, Google's, now Alphabet's, Debug initiative has asked the federal government for permission to release up to 32 million sterilized male mosquitoes in California and Florida.

items.title: Over 10,000 traps reveal only 1 pookila in southeast Queensland survey
items.summary: It's 5 a.m. when we crawl out of our tents. The catbirds are calling and the night sky is fading to dawn as we prepare to head into the wilderness. We're surrounded by the towering eucalypts of southeast Queensland in search of a small, native mouse called the pookila.

items.title: Jurassic sea monster was smaller than once thought, but no less formidable
items.summary: For more than 25 years, Liopleurodon has been one of the world's most recognizable prehistoric marine predators. Immortalized in the BBC's landmark Walking with Dinosaurs series as a colossal 25-meter (82-foot) marine hunter, it captured the imagination of millions and became synonymous wi

items.title: Finding Venus zone planets with PLATO will show us how habitable planets evolve
items.summary: Dreams of finding a sort of Earth 2.0 motivate much of the thinking around the search for exoplanets. But there is more to exoplanet science than searching for potentially habitable worlds. Scientists need a deeper understanding of the overall exoplanet population to understand the cosmos.

items.title: Burnt wooden beams discovered in Jerusalem shed new light on the First Temple's destruction
items.summary: A remarkable discovery has been made at the Givati Parking Lot excavations in the City of David, within Jerusalem Walls National Park. Excavations led by the Israel Antiquities Authority and Tel Aviv University uncovered massive wooden beams that collapsed and burned during the destruction of the Fi

items.title: AI awareness in Nebraska differs by age, education, income
items.summary: Artificial intelligence is being adopted rapidly across industries, workplaces and personal use, with an estimated 1 in 4 Americans using it regularly, but there is a knowledge gap.

items.title: Scientists use dual-frequency images to explore black hole plasma physics
items.summary: The first-ever image of a black hole—a glowing, ring-like structure at the heart of galaxy M87—was unveiled to the world in a landmark moment in 2019. Now, researchers have taken the next big step—moving beyond capturing a picture of the black hole to understanding its physics through dual-frequency

items.summary: Paul StankusBrookhaven Science Associates  The scientific goal is to enable a new method for observing gravitational waves at low frequencies, based on the astrometric GW signature — gravitational waves passing by the Earth will cause a (very small) coordinated apparent motion of all sky

items.title: Mapping Alien Continents: Achieving Optical VLBI for Exoplanet Imaging
items.summary: Paul StankusBrookhaven Science Associates The scientific goal of the proposed work will be reconstructing the image, ie resolving surface features, of an Earth-like exoplanet around a nearby star as seen in visible light. The innovation is in two stages. First, the design of a new kind of nulli

items.title: Interworld Slingshot Resource Surveys
items.summary: Pablo Sobron SanchezSETI Institute This proposal explores a new class of reconnaissance spacecraft that map minerals from orbit using Raman spectroscopy during high-speed flybys–without landing, sample return, or extended dwell. If feasible, this concept would enable NASA to evaluate ice and i

items.title: Robotically Assembled Electromagnetic Metamaterials for Long-Range Space Situational Awareness
items.summary: David SmithDuke University  The increasing population of spacefaring vehicles and satellites motivates increasingly powerful technologies for space situational awareness (SSA). While the US space surveillance network (SSN) is able to monitor objects down to about four inches in size using,

items.title: Photophoretic Tracers for Near-Space Remote Sensing at 30-100 km Altitudes
items.summary: Benjamin SchaferRarified Technologies, Inc. We propose a new approach to atmospheric sensing at 30-100 km altitudes using photophoretically levitating tracers. items.summary: Michael RubensteinNorthwestern University, Chicago We propose a mission to use ~10,000 actively steerable femtosats to map the ring composition, atmospheric composition and density, and the magnetic field distribution of Saturn. items.title: Trust, connection are key ingredients for Hawaiʻi's food security
items.summary: When talking about Hawaiʻi's food security, the conversation often focuses on physical infrastructure—warehouses, cold storage and doubling crop production to reduce the need to import 90% of food. items.title: From quantum error correction to emergent gravity: Probing holographic universes at QLab
items.summary: One of the deepest ambitions in modern physics is understanding how the fabric of space and time could emerge from fundamental quantum degrees of freedom to establish a quantum theory of gravity.

items.title: Cosmic gymnastics: How the Milky Way once underwent a dramatic flip
items.summary: The Milky Way may have undergone a dramatic change in orientation during its history, according to new research that helps explain a long-standing mystery about our galaxy. items.title: New geopolitical-economic order framework shows firm strategies vary under two types of state-market order
items.summary: A new paper published in Global Strategy Journal develops a geopolitical-economic order framework suggesting that the extent of geopolitical competition incentivizes states to create one of two types of economic order: a market-based order built on laissez-faire and mutually beneficial voluntary exc

items.title: New NASA Earth missions gear up to start science flights
items.summary: Landslides in Alaska. Air quality in Atlanta. Fire clouds out West. From the Arctic fringes to farm country, NASA's newest class of suborbital Earth Venture missions is gearing up to deliver science that will benefit communities in the United States and beyond.

items.title: New 'demon' cavefish lineage discovered in Alabama cave
items.summary: For decades, scientists have crawled through Bobcat Cave on Redstone Arsenal in Huntsville, searching its dark pools and underground passages for rare life. The cave is home to the endangered Alabama cave shrimp, making it one of the most closely watched cave systems in the region. Researchers have

items.title: How community-led health and livelihood programs helped protect rainforests during the pandemic
items.summary: As COVID-19 disrupted rural economies, communities living near protected rainforests faced rising uncertainty about jobs, health and basic needs. items.title: Geophysics brings clarity to the potential of natural hydrogen
items.summary: Natural hydrogen is considered a potential low-carbon energy source. But how much is generated underground—and how quickly? items.title: Establishing Crew Exposure Limits of Martian Dust
items.summary: Background Human exploration of Mars will expose crews to a persistent, fine particulate environment whose physicochemical properties and health implications remain only partly understood. items.title: Listening in on toads reveals expansion secrets
items.summary: Analysis of more than 3 million toad calls from locations across Australia gives rare insights into toad breeding behavior—and offers new hope for halting their advance. items.title: New wasp species named after Ernest Hemingway amid efforts to control destructive sugar beet pest
items.summary: Earth remains full of undiscovered species and unexplored interactions that continue to fascinate us, including, in the most ordinary places, the fields where our food is grown.

items.title: A star orbiting our galaxy's supermassive black hole tests the limits of relativity
items.summary: Although general relativity (GR) is an elegant theory of gravity, we don't often need to use it. items.title: Watching too much TV in midlife may shrink the brain
items.summary: Watching television very often in midlife may be linked to troubling brain changes decades later. items.title: A fairer world without destroying the planet? It's possible
items.summary: Imagine a future where everyone has enough, on a planet that stays livable. A new report makes the case that it's within reach.

items.title: Triassic inferno: Fires raged across Europe for millennia during past global warming
items.summary: The end-Triassic mass extinction, 201 million years ago, is linked to volcanism that resulted from the break-up of Pangea and which pumped out vast amounts of CO2 into the atmosphere, causing extreme global warming of 5 to 10 degrees Celsius.
